1.synthesis: 'Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more. And I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband' (vv.1–2). A loud voice from the throne announces the covenant's ultimate fulfillment: 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people … He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away' (vv.3–4). The one seated on the throne speaks directly: 'Behold, I am making all things new' and 'It is done. I am the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end' (vv.5–6). The victor inherits all things; the cowardly and defiled face the second death (vv.7–8). Revelation 21 is the Bible's final answer to Genesis 3: creation is not annihilated but transformed, the covenant dwelling-promise is permanently fulfilled, and every consequence of the fall is reversed.
1.historical_context: The new heaven and earth directly fulfills Isaiah 65:17 and Isaiah 66:22. The sea's disappearance (v.1) carries cosmological weight: in the ancient Near Eastern worldview the sea was primordial chaos, the abode of the dragon (Revelation 13:1); its absence signals the chaos-principle's final defeat. The new Jerusalem's descent 'from God out of heaven' (v.2) continues the heavenly-city tradition of Galatians 4:26 and Hebrews 11:10: the city arrives as divine gift, not human construction. The tabernacle-language of v.3 (skēnē) deliberately echoes the covenant promise that runs from Leviticus 26:11–12 through Ezekiel 37:27 and Jeremiah 31:33. The wiping of tears (v.4) quotes Isaiah 25:8. The 'It is done' of v.6 echoes both the seventh-bowl cry of 16:17 and the cross-cry of John 19:30 (tetelestai): what was finished at Calvary is enacted in the new creation.
1.literary_structure: The eight verses move in sequence: cosmological declaration (v.1), civic/bridal declaration (v.2), covenantal declaration (v.3), pastoral declaration (v.4), divine self-declaration (vv.5–6, 'all things new'; Alpha and Omega), victor's inheritance (v.7), excluded catalog (v.8). The bride-city of v.2 creates the structural counterpoint to harlot-Babylon of chapters 17–18: both are cities personified as women, but the bride descends from God while the harlot arose from the beast. The Alpha-Omega of v.6 exactly brackets the divine self-declaration at the book's opening (1:8): the God who declared himself Beginning-and-End at the vision's start now declares it at its completion.
1.theological_application: Gill on the new heaven and earth: 'the present heavens and earth are to continue, though purged by fire, that they may be fitted for the pure and holy inhabitants of them … it is not a disappearance of them, so as to be no more, but a change of quality, and not of substance.' On the sea's absence: the sea may signify 'the multitude of the wicked, compared to the sea for their number, noise, and turbulence: these shall be no more, for they will all be in the lake of fire and brimstone.' Barnes on v.3: 'The most exalted conception that we can form of future blessedness is that of dwelling with God, of having him in our immediate presence, and of knowing that he is our God.' Matthew Henry on vv.3–4: 'He will be their God, and they shall be his people — the covenant fully accomplished … The elimination of the fourfold curse — death, mourning, crying, pain — is the precise reversal of the consequences of the fall that run through human history from Genesis 3 onward.'
1.christology: The new creation is the eschatological consequence of the atonement: the 'It is done' of v.6 echoes Christ's tetelestai (John 19:30) — what was finished at the cross is fulfilled in the new earth. The Alpha-and-Omega of v.6 is the same title applied to Christ in 22:13, making Father and Son together the source and telos of the new creation. The bride descending (v.2) is the Lamb's bride, announced at 19:7: the new Jerusalem is the community purchased by his blood. The free gift of the water of life to the thirsty (v.6) echoes John 4:14 and John 7:37–38 — the Christ who offered living water to the Samaritan and at the feast now offers it eschatologically from the source itself.

9.synthesis: 'Come, I will show you the Bride, the wife of the Lamb.' And he carried me away in the Spirit to a great, high mountain, and showed me the holy city Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from God, having the glory of God, its radiance like a most rare jewel, like a jasper, clear as crystal' (vv.9–11). The extended vision occupies the next ten verses: twelve gates bearing the names of Israel's twelve tribes with twelve angels at the gates (vv.12–13), twelve foundation stones bearing the names of the twelve apostles (v.14), the city's dimensions as a perfect cube of 12,000 stadia (v.16), the wall's 144 cubits (v.17), and a cascade of precious stones adorning the foundations and pearl gates (vv.18–21). The perfect cube of v.16 is the vision's architectural climax — it directly echoes the Holy of Holies in Solomon's Temple, twenty cubits in each direction (1 Kings 6:20). The entire New Jerusalem is a Holy of Holies at cosmological scale: where formerly only the high priest could enter the divine presence once a year, the whole city is the zone of unmediated presence.
9.historical_context: The guiding angel — 'one of the seven angels who had the seven bowls' — is the same type as the one who showed John harlot Babylon in Revelation 17:1. The parallelism is deliberate: the same bowl-angel who revealed the harlot-city now reveals the bride-city, making the two visions mirror inversions. The Spirit-transport to the high mountain echoes Ezekiel 40:2 precisely — Ezekiel's vision of the restored city (chapters 40–48) is the primary OT template. Twelve gates named for the twelve tribes fulfills Ezekiel 48:30–34. Twelve foundation stones bearing apostolic names fulfills Ephesians 2:20. The twelve precious stones on the foundations echo the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17–20) — the city's foundations carry the same representational function as the breastplate, presenting all of God's people before him. The combination of twelve-tribe gates and twelve-apostle foundations unites the two epochs of covenant history — Israel and the church — as one eschatological structure.
9.literary_structure: The section moves from macro to micro: city-as-whole (glory and radiance, vv.10–11) → perimeter (gates and foundations, vv.12–14) → dimensions (measurement by golden reed, vv.15–17) → specific materials (jasper wall, gold city, foundation stones, pearl gates, vv.18–21). The number twelve dominates: twelve gates, twelve angels, twelve tribes, twelve foundations, twelve apostles, twelve thousand stadia, twelve squared as wall measurement, twelve foundation stones, twelve pearl gates. The numerical pattern of twelve and twelve-squared is the city's architectural DNA, evoking the totality of the covenant people. The cube-shape of v.16 is the section's theological apex: equal in all three dimensions, it is the form of the sanctuary of God's direct presence, now extended to cosmological scale.
9.theological_application: Gill on the city as the church: 'This city is not to be literally understood of a city of gold, silver, and precious stones, but figuratively; and by it is meant the church and people of God in the glorified state.' On the Bride identification (v.9): 'called "the Lamb's wife", because of the near and strict union there is between Christ and his church; she is the spouse of Christ, and he is the bridegroom, as the covenant of grace is a marriage covenant between them.' On the cube-shape: 'It is a figure of the perfection and completeness of the state of the glorified church, equal in all its dimensions, firm and stable in all respects.' Matthew Henry on gates and foundations: 'The church has its foundations in the doctrine, authority, and power of the apostles; and it has its open gates, through which all have access who have right to enter — the twelve gates suggest that there are enough entrances for all, from every nation and tribe.' Victorinus of Pettau (on v.1): 'the city which he says is squared, he says also is resplendent with gold and precious stones, and has a sacred street, and a river through the midst of it, and the tree of life on either side' — connecting the architectural details to the fuller Paradise-restoration of Revelation 22.
9.christology: The city is explicitly 'the Bride, the wife of the Lamb' (v.9) — not merely the city of God but the Lamb's city, the community purchased by his blood. The apostolic names on the twelve foundations (v.14) represent witnesses to the Lamb's identity, fulfilling Ephesians 2:20 ('with Christ Jesus himself as the cornerstone'). The perfect cube of v.16 — the Holy of Holies at cosmological scale — fulfills what the cross achieved: the torn veil of Matthew 27:51 opened the way into the divine presence; the new Jerusalem is that presence extended permanently to all who are washed in the Lamb's blood. The twelve-tribe gates and twelve-apostle foundations together constitute the city as Christ's full covenant community — the builder of the church he promised (Matthew 16:18), now in its consummated form.

22.pericope_label: The Glory of the New Jerusalem
22.synthesis: 'And I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb. And the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb. By its light will the nations walk, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it, and its gates will never be shut by day — and there will be no night there. They will bring into it the glory and the honor of the nations. But nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false, but only those who are written in the Lamb's book of life' (vv.22–27). Three absences define the new Jerusalem's character: no temple (v.22), no sun or moon (v.23), no night (v.25). Each absence is an arrival. No temple means God and the Lamb are themselves the sanctuary — unmediated presence has replaced mediated presence. No sun or moon means uncreated light (God's glory; the Lamb as lamp) has superseded created light. No night means the gates stand perpetually open for the nations, and nothing of darkness can enter. The inclusion criterion closes the chapter: only those written in the Lamb's book of life (v.27).
22.historical_context: The absence of a temple reverses the entire OT trajectory — tabernacle → Solomon's temple → Zerubbabel's temple → Herod's temple → Ezekiel's eschatological temple (Ezek 40–48) — because the entire city is now the sanctuary. 'The Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple' (v.22): the mediation-structure the temple provided has been replaced by the direct persons of Father and Son. The no-sun, no-moon vision fulfills Isaiah 60:19–20 ('the LORD will be your everlasting light'). Nations walking by the city's light fulfills Isaiah 60:3; the kings bringing their glory fulfills Isaiah 60:5, 11. Isaiah 60 is Jerusalem's great oracle of eschatological glory, and every element of that oracle arrives here. The never-shut gates fulfill Isaiah 60:11. The holiness-exclusion of v.27 echoes Isaiah 52:1 and Zechariah 14:21.
22.literary_structure: The six verses open with two absences that define what the city IS (no temple, because God and Lamb are the temple; no sun/moon, because divine glory is the light, vv.22–23), then describe what flows through the city by that light (nations walk; kings bring glory; honor of nations enters, vv.24–26), and close with the exclusion criterion (only those in the Lamb's book of life, v.27). The contrast between the 'glory and honor of the nations' entering (v.26) and 'nothing unclean' ever entering (v.27) is not a contradiction: what nations bring as tribute is the redeemed best of human civilization; what cannot enter is corruption, shame, and falsehood. The kings who once lamented Babylon (18:9) now bring their glory to the Lamb's city — the arc from lament to worship is the chapter's implicit subplot.
22.theological_application: Gill on v.22: 'And I saw no temple therein — No material temple … nor any place of public worship, as under the Gospel dispensation; for in this state there will be no such external form of worship as now, such as preaching the word and administering ordinances … the Lord God Almighty, and the Lamb, are the temple of it; they are the substance, the antitype of the temple, of which that was a shadow; God in Christ, and Christ as God and man, will be the temple in which his people will eternally worship and enjoy him.' On v.23: 'By the light of God, or rather the glory of God, is meant the divine Being himself … the Shechinah will be there.' Barnes on v.24: 'The idea seems to be that the splendors of all lands and all ages will be concentrated there; all that is beautiful in a landscape; all that is gorgeous in buildings; all that is rich in gold and gems.' Matthew Henry: 'The heavenly Jerusalem will be enlightened, not by the sun and moon, but by the glory of God and the Lamb. God himself will be the sun of that world; his glory will be the light of it' — the direct fulfillment of the Aaronic blessing ('the LORD make his face shine on you', Numbers 6:25) given immediately, not filtered through created luminaries.
22.christology: 'Its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb' (v.22) and 'its lamp is the Lamb' (v.23) — the Lamb is placed in exact parallel with the Almighty as jointly constituting the city's temple, and he is the lamp through which God's glory is mediated. The lamp-image for the Lamb reaches back to Revelation 1:12–20, where Christ walks among the seven lampstands: the one who was the lamp of the seven churches in history is the lamp of the new Jerusalem in eternity. The nations walking by the city's light (v.24) fulfills John 8:12 ('I am the light of the world') at eschatological scale. The kings bringing their glory to the city bring it to the Lamb — the one to whom every knee bows (Philippians 2:10). The Lamb's book of life (v.27) is the city's inclusion criterion because the Lamb is its living center: to have one's name written there is to belong to the one who is both its lamp and its temple.
